title: Rust 贡献者探索 AI 辅助编译器开发：推出全新 Rue 语言
author: Steef-Jan Wiggers
content:
Steve Klabnik, known for authoring The Rust Programming Language and for his contributions to the Rust project over 13 years, has announced Rue, a systems programming language that explores memory safety without garbage collection while prioritizing developer ergonomics over Rust's complexity. The project, developed with substantial assistance from Anthropic's Claude AI, targets an underserved design space between high-performance systems languages and garbage-collected alternatives.

Marking his thirteenth anniversary using Rust, Klabnik explained his motivation in a blog post:

I have long been wondering if I should try and make my own language. I really like them! That's part of why I got involved in Ruby and then Rust in the first place!

The language name follows his "Ru" prefix pattern (Ruby, Rust, Rue) while maintaining dual interpretations—both a flower and an expression of regret.

Klabnik's core design question: "What if Rust wasn't trying to compete with C and C++ for the highest performance possible? What if we were willing to make things a little bit, but not too much, less performant, in exchange for ease of use?"

The technical approach centers on eliminating Rust's signature feature, the borrow checker. Consider typical Rust code where you try to modify a vector while holding a reference to one of its elements. The compiler rejects this because the reference might become invalid. Rue sidesteps the entire problem by using "inout" parameters that temporarily transfer ownership, similar to Swift. In Rust, attempting to modify a vector while iterating over it fails at compile time. Rue's inout parameters let you temporarily pass mutable references, but prevent storing them in data structures; eliminating the need for lifetime tracking while maintaining memory safety through simpler restrictions.

Functions can mutate values in place, but those values can't be stored as references in heap-allocated structures. No lifetime annotations needed. The tradeoff? Certain patterns become impossible to express. As the design documentation acknowledges, Rue can't support iterators that borrow from their containers; they have to consume them instead.

The Hacker News community responded with both interest and skepticism. One commenter captured the challenge:

Rust has only succeeded in making a Memory-Safe Language without garbage collection by introducing significant complexity (which was a trade-off). No one really knows a sane way to do it otherwise, unless you also want to drop the general-purpose systems programming language requirement.

According to design proposals in the GitHub repository, Rue implements four distinct ownership modes: value types, affine types, linear types, and reference-counted types. Klabnik acknowledged in responses that "there is going to inherently be some expressiveness loss. There is no silver bullet."

The development methodology represents an experiment addressing a question Klabnik has pondered for years: "Without funding or a team, can a single person still build a programming language?" The approach marks a shift for Klabnik, who described himself as an AI skeptic until 2025. His first attempt at building Rue without effectively leveraging AI had to be abandoned after months of work. This iteration, using Anthropic's Claude AI more effectively, produced approximately 70,000 lines of working Rust compiler code in two weeks, far outpacing his previous multi-month attempt.

The collaboration goes beyond typical coding assistance. In blog posts co-credited to both Klabnik and Claude, the AI described writing most of the implementation code while Klabnik directed the architecture and made design decisions. Klabnik emphasized that effectively using AI tools requires substantial skill: "Simply knowing how to write code isn't actually enough to truly use large models well. They are a new category of tools in their own right." His approach involved iterative experimentation, writing short code snippets, starting conversations, and testing different prompting strategies. Whether this model can eliminate the need for the substantial investments that historically funded language projects remains to be seen.

Rue remains in early development with basic control flow, functions, and non-generic enums. It compiles to native executables via custom backends rather than LLVM, achieving fast compile times through simplified semantics. Heap allocation is in progress, while Language Server Protocol support, package management, and concurrency models are not yet implemented. The project uses Buck2 rather than Cargo for future compiler bootstrapping.

Klabnik maintains modest expectations: "I don't expect it to grow to anything more than my hobby project." Still, he noted that Rasmus Lerdorf and Graydon Hoare, creators of PHP and Rust, also started with personal experiments.

The experiment comes as AI-assisted development tools reshape software engineering. While GitHub Copilot and similar tools assist with incremental coding, Klabnik's approach using AI for architectural-scale work on a compiler represents a different level of collaboration. If successful, it could suggest that complex infrastructure projects traditionally requiring large teams might become feasible for skilled individuals with AI assistance.

The real test will be whether developers frustrated by Rust's learning curve but unwilling to adopt garbage collection find Rue's tradeoffs acceptable. As one Hacker News commenter put it:

I'll be very interested if they find a new unexplored point in the design space, but at the moment, I remain skeptical.

The Rue language documentation is available at rue-lang.dev, and the source code is on GitHub.
aiSummary: Rust 资深成员 Steve Klabnik 正在开发 “Rue”，这是一种全新的系统语言，旨在通过 AI 驱动的编译器工程实现无需借用检查器或垃圾回收的内存安全。
oneSentenceSummary: Rust 资深成员 Steve Klabnik 正在开发 “Rue”，这是一种全新的系统语言，旨在通过 AI 驱动的编译器工程实现无需借用检查器或垃圾回收的内存安全。
summary: Rust 社区知名人物 Steve Klabnik 推出了 Rue，这是一种系统编程语言，旨在弥合高性能 C++/Rust 与易用的垃圾回收语言之间的差距。Rue 目标是通过利用 “inout” 参数和受限的所有权模式来实现内存安全，无需借用检查器，虽然牺牲了一定的表达力，但换取了易用性。值得注意的是，该项目是 AI 辅助开发的一个里程碑式实验；利用 Anthropic 的 Claude，Klabnik 在短短两周内生成了 70，000 行编译器代码。这种方法预示着一种转变：传统上需要大型团队协作的复杂基础设施项目，现在可以由个人利用 AI 进行架构级实现。

mainPoints.point: Rue 在高性能系统语言与 GC 替代方案之间探索了一个独特的设计空间。
mainPoints.explanation: 通过移除借用检查器并使用 “inout” 参数，Rue 寻求提供比 Rust 认知负荷更低的内存安全，尽管这会损失一定的表达力。

mainPoints.point: AI 可以大幅加速编译器等复杂基础设施的开发。
mainPoints.explanation: Klabnik 利用 Claude 在两周内产出了 70，000 行代码，证明了在资深架构师的指导下，AI 可以处理大规模的实现工作。

mainPoints.point: 有效利用 AI 是一项独立于传统编程的特殊技能。
mainPoints.explanation: 该项目强调，“提示工程” 以及与 LLM 的迭代协作是实现从增量帮助转向架构级工作所需的新工程学科。

mainPoints.point: 该项目挑战了语言开发必须依赖大型团队的必要性。
mainPoints.explanation: Rue 作为一个案例研究，探讨了在 AI 的增强下，单个专家是否能够构建出历史上需要大量机构资金和人力投入的工具。

keyQuotes: 如果 Rust 不再试图与 C 和 C++ 竞争极致性能会怎样？如果我们愿意为了易用性而牺牲一点点（但不是太多）性能，结果会如何？
仅仅知道如何编写代码实际上并不足以真正用好大模型。它们本身就是一类全新的工具。
AI 编写了大部分实现代码，而 Klabnik 则负责指导架构并做出设计决策。
表达力必然会有所损失。世上没有银弹。
如果取得成功，这可能意味着传统上需要大型团队的复杂基础设施项目，对于有 AI 辅助的资深个人来说也将变得可行。
